did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a convolutional neural network (CNN) for the classification of malignant melanoma from dermoscopy images. The CNN's configuration is optimized using an improved African Vulture Optimization algorithm (a metaheuristic AI method). The study was conducted on the SIIM-ISIC Melanoma and HAM10000 datasets to achieve computer-aided diagnosis results.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'RESEARCH ARTICLE' in the header. The abstract and introduction describe the proposal of a new procedure for melanoma diagnosis using a combined convolutional neural network and an improved African Vulture Optimization algorithm. The paper details original experimental work, including data preprocessing, model design, optimization of CNN parameters, and validation on the SIIM-ISIC Melanoma dataset, comparing results with other approaches.

title: A computer-aided melanoma detection using deep learning and an improved African vulture optimization algorithm
journal: International Journal of Imaging Systems and Technology
brief_summary: This study proposes a computer-aided diagnosis system for malignant melanoma using a convolutional neural network (CNN) optimized by an improved African Vulture Optimization algorithm. The method employs Kapur segmentation and mathematical morphology for preprocessing dermoscopy images to extract key features. The model was validated on the SIIM-ISIC Melanoma and HAM10000 datasets, with performance evaluated against several existing approaches using metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, and accuracy. The proposed CNN-MEFOA method achieved an accuracy of 92.7% on the SIIM-ISIC dataset and 86.99% on the HAM10000 dataset. These results demonstrated superior performance compared to other recent methods in terms of sensitivity and overall accuracy. The authors conclude that while the method is highly effective, future work will focus on simplifying its configuration to reduce computational complexity.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on the detection and diagnosis of malignant melanoma using dermoscopy images from the SIIM-ISIC Melanoma and HAM10000 datasets. The classification task distinguishes between melanoma (positive) and healthy skin/non-melanoma cases (negative), as indicated by metrics like sensitivity (correctly detected skin cancer cases) and specificity (correctly detected healthy skin cases).

Used as the negative class for binary classification against melanoma. The paper mentions distinguishing between 'malignant and benign lesions' in the context of dermoscopy capabilities and uses 'healthy skin cases' in the calculation of specificity.

all_task_types.target: Malignant melanoma vs. non-melanoma (healthy skin/benign lesions)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to diagnose malignant melanoma from dermoscopy images. The CNN predicts discrete diagnostic categories (melanoma vs. non-melanoma), evaluated using metrics such as sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, PPV, and NPV on the SIIM-ISIC Melanoma and HAM10000 datasets.

datasets_used.name: SIIM-ISIC Melanoma dataset
datasets_used.description: A popular dataset containing dermoscopy images of melanoma, downloaded from Kaggle (https://www.kaggle.com/c/siim-isic-melanoma-classification). Images are in DICOM format with uniform 1024x1024 size and include metadata.

use.training: Used for training the CNN model (85% of the data was applied to train).
use.testing: Used for testing the model (15% of the data was applied to test).

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: Also known as 'Human Against Machine with 10010 training images', this is a large dataset with 10,000 dermatoscopic skin images with different modalities collected from various populations. Ground truth was established by expert consensus, follow-up examination, or in vivo confocal microscopy. Downloaded from Kaggle (https://www.kaggle.com/kmader/skin-cancer-mnist-ham10000).

use.training: Used for training the CNN model (85% of the data was applied to train).
use.testing: Used for testing the model (15% of the data was applied to test).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To account for the stochastic nature of the hybrid metaheuristic deep learning methodology and provide more consistent results by calculating mean and standard deviation values.

To evaluate and compare the performance of the proposed IAVOA algorithm against other optimization algorithms (AVOA, BH, MVO, EPO, LOA) on benchmark functions, and to report the consistency of results across the 15 independent runs.

statistical_methods_used.method: Comparison of Minimum Value (MD) and Standard Deviation
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To validate the effectiveness of the IAVOA algorithm by comparing its minimum objective function values and variability against other algorithms on standard benchmark functions (Rastrigin, Rosenbrock, Ackley, Sphere).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). Performance was evaluated using descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation) over 15 independent runs and comparative analysis of minimum values against other algorithms.

SIIM-ISIC Melanoma dataset.notes: The proposed CNN-MEFOA method achieved the highest accuracy (92.7%) and sensitivity (96.3%) among the compared methods on the SIIM-ISIC dataset.

HAM10000.notes: The proposed CNN-MEFOA method achieved the highest accuracy (86.99%) and sensitivity (92.99%) among the compared methods on the HAM10000 dataset.

hyperparameters_optimized.sliding_window_size: max value (specific number not reported, described as 'max value')

architecture_details.notes: The CNN structure was optimized using IAVOA. The specific layer count (L) and number of individuals (A) were treated as variables to be optimized. The optimization aimed to minimize the objective function related to half-value accuracy.

training_details.hardware: 64-bit Intel Core i7 CPU with 2.00GHz and 8 GB RAM

preprocessing_techniques: Histogram equalization for contrast enhancement and image quality improvement
Noise removal using a method that processes and clusters the local histogram followed by pixel recovery to separate noise pixels from healthy pixels
Kapur segmentation (thresholding based on entropy maximization and histogram information) to extract key features and eliminate adverse effects
Mathematical morphology including opening (to remove tiny details) and closing (to remove holes or thin lines attached to large objects)
conclusions: The proposed CNN-MEFOA method, which combines a convolutional neural network optimized by an improved African Vulture Optimization algorithm, achieved an accuracy of 92.7% on the SIIM-ISIC Melanoma dataset and 86.99% on the HAM10000 dataset.

The proposed method demonstrated superior performance compared to existing approaches (Kumar's, Jain's, Zghal's, Shrivastava's, and Kadampur's) in terms of sensitivity and overall accuracy on both datasets.
On the SIIM-ISIC dataset, the CNN-MEFOA method achieved a sensitivity of 96.3% and specificity of 94.1%.
On the HAM10000 dataset, the CNN-MEFOA method achieved a sensitivity of 92.99% and specificity of 90.99%.
The study acknowledges that traditional CNN methods have disadvantages such as higher computational cost, the need for high-GPU configuration, and the requirement for large amounts of training data.
The authors conclude that future work should focus on simplifying the configuration of the method to reduce computational complexity.
important_remarks: The study relies on retrospective public datasets (SIIM-ISIC and HAM10000) downloaded from Kaggle, lacking external clinical validation or prospective data collection.
The paper does not report the total number of images or the class distribution (per-class counts) for the SIIM-ISIC dataset, limiting assessment of class imbalance.
The HAM10000 dataset includes ground truth established by expert consensus, follow-up, or confocal microscopy, introducing potential label noise or heterogeneity in the 'benign' class.

The data splitting ratio (85% training, 15% testing) was selected via 'trials and errors' rather than a standardized protocol or cross-validation, raising concerns about data leakage or overfitting to the specific split.
The authors acknowledge that the proposed CNN-MEFOA method has a 'highly complicated configuration' and high computational cost, noting that future work will focus on simplification.
The study was conducted on standard hardware (Intel Core i7 CPU, 8 GB RAM) using MATLAB 2017b, which may not reflect performance on typical GPU-accelerated clinical deployment environments.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; performance comparisons were based on descriptive statistics (mean and standard deviation) over 15 runs.
The paper does not report data augmentation techniques, which may limit the model's robustness to variations in dermoscopy images.
The preprocessing steps (Kapur segmentation, mathematical morphology) are applied before the CNN, which may discard subtle diagnostic features compared to end-to-end deep learning approaches.
